title: MAGA's attack on science is even worse than it looks
gist: Smith argues the postwar US science system — a key institutional engine of American economic and military dominance — is being subjected to an unprecedented Trump-administration attack (funding cuts, ideological review), and that the right's justification that Americans have lost trust in science is a myth: polls show most Americans, including two-thirds of Republicans, still trust scientists and support more science spending.

themes.theme: Science as an engine of US dominance

insights.claim: Much of America's modern science infrastructure — the research university, government science grants, public-to-private research spinoffs — did not exist before WWII and was invented or massively scaled by the US government in the postwar period under scientist-bureaucrats like Vannevar Bush.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Frames federal science funding as load-bearing national infrastructure, not discretionary spend; degrading it threatens the industrial base.
insights.locator: Today we take things like the modern research university

insights.claim: Science was the driver behind American industry — chemicals, aerospace, telecommunications, computers, electronics — making the scientific enterprise directly responsible for US jobs, living standards, and great-power status.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Ties the US industrial and defense edge to a science base now under threat, implying long-horizon competitiveness risk.
insights.quote: Science was also the driver behind American industry
insights.locator: Science gave us radar and the atom bomb

insights.claim: China's science spending and output have overtaken the US by some measures due to its incredible growth and investment, but Smith argues China's rise is net-positive for American science since US scientists can build on Chinese discoveries for free.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Counters zero-sum framing of Chinese science; the real risk is talent flight to China and military applications, not the loss of knowledge spillovers.
insights.quote: overall, China's rise in science should be good for American science, since American scientists can use Chinese discoveries for free and build on them
insights.locator: America's scientific enterprise is still strong

insights.claim: A concrete downside of China's scientific rise is that top scientists can now choose to work in China instead of the US, alongside the risk that Chinese technological strength bolsters its military.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Talent competition is the binding constraint; US self-inflicted funding cuts amplify the pull of Chinese labs.
insights.locator: There are a few ways in which China's rise creates problems

insights.claim: The right-wing claim that scientists have lost the trust of regular Americans is false: a late-2025 Pew poll shows that while about a fifth of Republicans lost confidence in 2020, two-thirds still have at least a fair amount of confidence, and scientists rank among America's most trusted groups — more trusted than the military.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: The political justification for science cuts lacks a popular mandate; trust erosion is confined to a MAGA minority, not the electorate.
insights.quote: scientists are among America's most trusted groups — even better trusted than the military!

insights.claim: Even where Republican trust in science dropped somewhat, Republican support for spending more on science remains very strong, per Burgess (2026) and a 2023 Pew poll.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Science funding cuts run against even Republican voters' stated preferences, a political vulnerability for the administration.
insights.locator: Other polls show that although Republican trust

insights.claim: Smith concedes progressives did politicize scientific research during the Biden administration and made real missteps, arguing objectivity matters for long-term public trust, but insists there is currently no crisis of trust in science.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: A balanced framing that separates a real long-run objectivity concern from the false immediate-crisis narrative used to justify cuts.
insights.quote: there's no crisis of trust in science, except among the smallish minority of people who are running around screaming that there's a crisis of trust in science
insights.locator: That doesn't mean I think progressives

insights.claim: Despite science's popularity, the Trump administration is launching an unprecedented attack on American science — cutting funding and forcing science projects to undergo ideological review by government commissars — threatening the system with absolute decline.
insights.speaker: Noah Smith
insights.so_what: Ideological review plus funding cuts is a structural, not cyclical, hit to US research capacity with multi-decade competitiveness consequences.
insights.quote: forcing science projects to undergo ideological review by government commissars
